title: Farmhouse Kitchen Island
summary: A chunky 4x4-legged island with two side-mount drawers and an open bottom shelf — real casework scaled for a kitchen, not a small side project.
description:
This island's legs and aprons form the real structure — 4x4 posts joined by 1x8 side aprons and 2x8 top supports — with the drawer box and bottom shelf built into that frame rather than as a separate cabinet dropped inside it. The spacers between the legs and side aprons matter more than they look: their position is different on each leg (not symmetric), so track which leg gets which spacer placement rather than assuming they're interchangeable.

The center divider between the two drawers is the single measurement that determines whether this island works well for years or annoys you every time you open a drawer — it has to go in dead square, because a divider that's even slightly out of square will bind every drawer that rides against it. Build and square that piece before you touch the drawers themselves.

tools.note: For ripping trim pieces and cutting the plywood shelf and top.
Squaring the center drawer divider is the single most important check in this build.

materials.name: 1x4 board, 8 ft
materials.note: Ripped down for the spacers and face trim as well as used at full width for the drawer boxes.

cutList.note: Position differs on each leg — do not treat as interchangeable.
Notch the corners to fit around the legs.
Cut to fit your actual drawer openings.

steps.title: Attach spacers and side aprons
steps.body: Attach the spacers to the legs — note the spacers are not in the same position on every leg, so track which leg is which as you go. Then fasten the side aprons to the spacers.

steps.title: Attach the top supports
steps.body: Use 3" screws to fasten the top supports (D) to the legs, working from the cut list dimensions — there's an intentional 1-3/4" difference on the sides you should account for.

steps.title: Attach the back apron and bottom supports
steps.body: Use 2" screws and glue to fasten the back apron in place, checking square as you go. Fasten the bottom front and back supports (H) to the side supports; if you plan to load the bottom shelf with heavy items, add a couple of 2x2 supports (F) across between the H boards for extra strength.

steps.title: Add the bottom shelf
steps.body: Notch the corners of the bottom shelf plywood to fit around the legs, then set it in place and fasten it down.

steps.title: Install the center drawer divider
steps.body: Using the third 2x8 board (D), fasten the center divider in place with 3" screws. This board must be dead square — any lean will keep your drawers from gliding smoothly for the life of the island.

steps.title: Add the face trim and top
steps.body: Fasten the horizontal face trim (L) with 2" fasteners, making sure it's fastened to the center divider (confirm it hasn't shifted from the last step), then fasten the vertical trim (M). Fasten the top in place — attaching to the trim, legs, aprons, and the 2x8 supports — centered with a 1" overhang on all sides. You may prefer to add the top after the drawers are built and fitted.

steps.title: Build and fit the drawers
steps.body:
Read your drawer slide instructions before building — this plan assumes side-mount slides needing 1/2" clearance per side. Cut the plywood bottoms perfectly straight, then fasten the 1x4 fronts and backs to the bottoms, followed by the sides. Fit each drawer box into its opening using the slides.

Once the boxes are set, measure and cut the drawer faces, marking and lightly scoring the center of each to suggest two drawers if you like that look. Attach the faces with a couple of 1-1/4" nails and glue, avoiding where pulls will go, then pull the drawers back out and drive screws from inside into the face for a permanent hold.

images.alt: Farmhouse kitchen island with two drawers and an open bottom shelf
